event: Early human hunter-gatherers leave footprints later preserved as the Acahualinca tracks near present-day Managua, marking among the oldest evidence of human presence in Nicaragua.
Agricultural societies cultivating maize, beans, and squash develop across the Pacific lowlands of what is now western Nicaragua.
Chibcha-speaking peoples and other groups establish settled villages and chiefdoms throughout the region.
Nahua-speaking Nicarao and Chorotega peoples migrate southward from Mesoamerica, settling the fertile Pacific zone around the great lakes.
Christopher Columbus reaches the Caribbean coast of Nicaragua during his fourth voyage, marking the first European contact.
Gil González Dávila leads the first Spanish expedition into the interior, encountering the chief Nicarao for whom the country is named.
Francisco Hernández de Córdoba founds the cities of Granada and León, beginning permanent Spanish colonization.
The original city of León is destroyed by the eruption of the Momotombo volcano and is rebuilt at its present location.
English buccaneers and settlers expand influence over the Mosquito Coast, establishing the British-protected Miskito Kingdom.
Pirates including Henry Morgan repeatedly sack the wealthy colonial city of Granada via the San Juan River and Lake Nicaragua.
British forces attempting to seize the San Juan River are repelled, including a defense led by the heroine Rafaela Herrera at the El Castillo fortress.
Nicaragua declares independence from Spain as part of the broader Central American independence movement on September 15.
Nicaragua joins the United Provinces of Central America, a federation of the former Spanish provinces.
Nicaragua secedes from the disintegrating Federal Republic of Central America to become a fully independent sovereign state.
Britain seizes the port of San Juan del Norte (Greytown), heightening tensions over control of a potential interoceanic canal route.
The Clayton-Bulwer Treaty between Britain and the United States agrees neither power will exclusively control a Nicaraguan canal.
American filibuster William Walker seizes power and declares himself president of Nicaragua, reinstating slavery before being driven out.
A coalition of Central American armies defeats William Walker, and Managua is later established as the national capital to end the León-Granada rivalry.
The Treaty of Managua transfers nominal British control of the Mosquito Coast to Nicaragua while granting the Miskito limited autonomy.
Liberal general José Santos Zelaya seizes power and begins a modernizing, centralizing dictatorship.
Zelaya formally reincorporates the autonomous Mosquito Coast into the Nicaraguan state.
Zelaya is overthrown amid U.S. pressure following the execution of two Americans, opening an era of U.S. intervention.
United States Marines land to support a conservative government, beginning a long military occupation of Nicaragua.
The Bryan-Chamorro Treaty grants the United States exclusive rights to build a canal across Nicaragua.
Augusto César Sandino launches a guerrilla rebellion against the U.S. Marine occupation and the conservative government.
U.S. Marines withdraw, leaving the U.S.-trained National Guard under the command of Anastasio Somoza García.
Sandino is assassinated on Somoza's orders after agreeing to a peace settlement.
Anastasio Somoza García takes the presidency, founding a family dynasty that rules Nicaragua for over four decades.
Nicaragua becomes a founding member of the United Nations.
Anastasio Somoza García is assassinated by the poet Rigoberto López Pérez, and power passes to his sons.
The Sandinista National Liberation Front (FSLN) is founded to oppose the Somoza dictatorship.
A catastrophic earthquake devastates Managua, killing thousands, and the regime's embezzlement of relief aid fuels growing opposition.
The assassination of opposition journalist Pedro Joaquín Chamorro sparks a nationwide uprising against Somoza.
The Sandinista revolution triumphs as Anastasio Somoza Debayle flees, ending the Somoza dynasty and establishing a revolutionary government.
The United States begins funding and arming the Contra rebels to fight the Sandinista government.
Daniel Ortega is elected president in elections boycotted by parts of the opposition.
The International Court of Justice rules that U.S. support for the Contras violated international law, a verdict the U.S. ignores.
Nicaragua grants autonomy to its Caribbean coast regions, and the regional Esquipulas peace accords advance an end to the war.
Violeta Chamorro defeats Daniel Ortega in democratic elections, ending Sandinista rule and the Contra war.
Hurricane Mitch devastates Nicaragua, killing thousands and causing massive flooding and economic damage.
Daniel Ortega returns to the presidency through democratic elections, beginning a new and enduring period in power.
Nicaragua joins the Venezuelan-led ALBA regional alliance, securing oil subsidies and aid.
The National Assembly abolishes presidential term limits, allowing Ortega indefinite reelection, amid a stalled Chinese-backed canal project.
Mass anti-government protests erupt and are violently suppressed, leaving hundreds dead and triggering international condemnation.
Daniel Ortega wins another term after jailing leading opposition candidates, drawing widespread accusations of an illegitimate election.
The government strips citizenship from hundreds of dissidents and expels political prisoners, deepening Nicaragua's authoritarian isolation.
